data.title: POET Technologies: The Missing Piece In Marvell's AI Empire
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Previously, in my articles, I referred to POET Technologies (POET) as a "rough diamond" that's gaining value due to the potential acquisition by Marvell Technology (MRVL). Currently, there's indirect confirmation of this thesis, because after the completion of the acquisition of Celestial AI, MRVL is gaining access to key technologies in which the POET team played a direct role. It's my view that POET’s role could become even more significant, shaping it as a player in the global market for chip photonics, the use of which is critical for data centers. The goal of this article is to analyze the role of POET’s technologies for MRVL, reveal its ambitious plans, evaluate the company’s potential value following successful commercialization, and assess the value of the first major $5 million contract. In my estimation, POET has the potential for market capitalization growth of up to $5 billion.

Previous Points

My thesis in previous articles about POET was that it's a "diamond in the rough" that could become a target for acquisition by Marvell. Starting in 2022, the name POET has been repeatedly mentioned in Celestial AI’s financial reports as a technology customer. Subsequently, together with POET, it collaborated on the development of an optical interposer platform, providing cutting-edge solutions for ensuring high computational efficiency in AI infrastructure. Since MRVL announced in December 2025 that it would acquire Celestial AI for $3.25 billion, this technology became available to it. Now that Marvell is transforming into an AI giant, the management and shareholders are interested in utilizing the best technologies used in data centers. Because POET has the technological capabilities to offer specific solutions based on its experience with Celestial AI, it strengthens the company's ties with MRVL. My rating was therefore "Strong Buy," based on my confidence that my predictions will meet optimistic expectations.

POET's Strategy

POET's strategy is built on finding customers to whom it can offer solutions for improving the energy efficiency of computing. The AI supercycle continues due to both the expansion of the GPU fleet and increased energy consumption. Basically, components that have been manufactured on the market to date are not fully adapted to operate at 1.6T speeds. Outdated technologies lead to overheating, an increase in energy consumption, and a decrease in the efficiency of computing operations. Because of this, the data centers are forced to increase their capex on cooling systems. On the other hand, POET offers an optical interposer which eliminates these problems and reduces cooling costs and power consumption.

Blazar is the company's flagship product (formed on a single chip, minimizing the space required for deployment in data centers), providing a ready-made module capable of operating at 1.6Т. The technology is one of the first in the world to combine the advantages of photonics and electronics into a single device, giving the market a unique solution to boost computing efficiency and improve data transmission stability. Meanwhile, many other companies are developing prototypes, but POET has already deployed Semtech’s first 1.6T receiver for practical use. Management has previously stated that the product is currently going through qualification testing on the computing infrastructure of major cloud providers, so a successful test period will enable the company to secure its first major contracts in the future.

The Role of POET Technologies in MRVL’s Ambitions

Today’s excitement surrounding POET shares is largely driven by news of the company’s collaboration with MRVL. POET’s CFO said the company has received an order from MRVL (shipment of products, followed by plans to continue and expand the partnership). Furthermore, additional orders are expected from Foxconn (FXCOF) and Luxshare Precision. These reports do not surprise me in the least, since I'm confident that POET’s technologies can be successfully and reliably integrated into Marvell’s ecosystem. Marvell's custom chips require high-quality photonics, exactly what POET offers. Main expectations that POET's technologies will be crucial for MRVL's ambitions emerged after the $3.25 billion acquisition of Celestial AI was finalized.

It turns out that Celestial AI and POET are in the same business, just in different segments. The former applies photonic technologies in systems that are directly integrated into data centers, whereas the latter develops these technologies to accelerate data transmission. Since 2022, information about POET being a client has repeatedly appeared in Celestial AI’s financial reports. Subsequently, they became partners in a project to jointly develop an optical interposer platform. MRVL acquired Celestial AI specifically to gain access to this technology, and therefore it's impossible to fully realize all of its plans without POET’s participation (as a contractor).

Marvell is currently successfully utilizing the Celestial AI Photonic Fabric in the development of its next-generation custom chips, integrated at 1.6T. As POET is working specifically on a solution for this type of challenge, they're an excellent option for sourcing photonic technologies to incorporate into new products. Naturally are not the only ones in this segment achieving excellent results in designing such technologies. The main competitors, of course, continue to be the market leaders in optical modules: Coherent (COHR) and Lumentum (LITE). For me, though, the main drawback of their business models, based on traditional discrete assembly of optical components, remains significant. Their production is more expensive, making it more difficult to scale than POET’s semiconductor-based method. Their technology is easier and cheaper to scale. It's only a question of POET’s own capabilities, and these still need to be developed and extended, but without major contracts, this is unlikely.

Expectations for the Q1 2026 Earnings Report

Based on Nasdaq estimates, this earnings report is expected to be released on May 13, 2026 (though I, personally, doubt this and expect the report closer to June, as the last report was released on March 31, 2026), for which analysts' consensus forecast anticipates a negative EPS of -0.05% and revenue of $250,000. That said, when we look at the last eight quarterly reports, every single one has delivered a negative surprise in terms of revenue, with EPS coming in below expectations 7 times. That tells us that forecasts aren’t holding up. However, things could change dramatically as early as the next quarterly report. Looking at Fintel's forecasts, they make a bold prediction of $13 million in revenue for POET in 2026, compared to $82 million in 2027.

We should also keep in mind that in November 2025, POET received a $5 million order to supply 800G optical modules. The CFO confirmed that these orders are crucial for expanding production capacity, and by the end of this year, the total order volume will significantly exceed $5 million. The attractiveness of POET for orders will continue to grow due to the growing demand for photonic technologies to address optical interconnect challenges in data centers.

As part of the next quarterly report, shareholders should pay attention to the following metrics:

  • Firstly, there will be a particular focus on finding evidence confirming that the order book is genuine and that the POET platform’s technological leadership is valid.
  • Secondly, it's possible that there will be official comments on how the company plans to implement its strategic move to eliminate its PFIC status, the existence of which creates barriers to share purchases by institutional investors. The relocation of the headquarters to the U.S. will minimize tax risks for U.S. investors, due to which POET is currently undervalued.
  • Finally, investors are keen to hear updates on the company’s collaborations with key partners. Further comments regarding the list of partners and the current status of these collaborations are particularly important. We will likely learn more about the fulfillment of the $5 million order and the scope of the partnership with MRVL—both now and in the future. The current interview with POET’s CFO serves, I believe, in part as a preview of what to expect in the next earnings report.

Valuation of POET

To assess POET’s investment appeal, we cannot use forward values of traditional multiples such as P/E, PEG, EV/sales, and EV/EBITDA. Essentially, the reason for this is the lack of reliable sales and profit figures. For this reason, my intention is to assess the company’s potential value by calculating its market share in the 1.6T segment. Based on Nvidia’s (NVDA) roadmap, in 2026 an average of approximately 6 million Rubin-based GPUs will be produced, using 1.5 transceivers per unit. Therefore, there will be a demand for approximately 9 million 1.6T transceiver ports, each with an average cost of $1,500. Thus, the transceiver market could be worth approximately $13.5 billion in 2026. But POET doesn’t sell finished transceivers; it sells optical engines, and these make up 25% of the total product cost (or $375).

Given that POET is projected to capture up to 10% of the total market share by 2027, sales could reach approximately 0.9 million optical engines. Reasons for this optimistic forecast include the use of semiconductor integration in product assembly, established partnerships with several major cloud providers, and the fact that shipments of 800G products under the first order have already begun. Based on an average product price of $375, sales of POET could reach $337.5 million by 2027.

Using COHR and LITE as examples, they have a median EV/Sales (fwd) multiple of 15.63x. This means that with sales of $337.5 million, a company's market value should be $5.275 billion. Taking into account POET's market capitalization of $1.57 billion at the time of writing, potential stock growth could reach 235%. I believe this is a realistic estimate looking ahead to the end of 2027, taking into account how highly volatile such assets are as they're aimed at participating in the AI supercycle. As an example, over the past year, the market value of LITE has increased 15-fold.

POET's Risk of a Disconnect Between Valuation and Actual Commercialization

POET stock is entering overbought territory, driven by investor expectations ahead of the next earnings report. It could become a key architect of the optical nervous system for AI, though it will need to get past the commercialization phase first. POET's main expenses are currently focused on R&D, not surprising since its core activities are still centered on research rather than production. The management plans to ship more than 30,000 modules in 2026, meaning that any delays in production, whether due to defects or issues with finished product output, may reduce investor confidence.

Moreover, this first major order worth $5 million highlights the company’s extreme revenue concentration. This is insufficient to cover its current losses. Markets are starting to price in more ideal execution of all future contracts, giving a premium to POET’s market value. Also, keep in mind that the company has set a tax trap for itself, so if the US relocation gets delayed, investors who bought shares might pull their money out. Therefore, even if the next earnings report doesn’t meet expectations, this could add weight to the arguments made by detractors.

Conclusion

Taking into account that POET shares have risen from $6.90 to the current $12.40 since my last recommendation (while my target was $15), I'm upgrading my rating from "Strong Buy" to "Buy." The sole purpose of this is to capitalize on volatility in order to purchase shares when the price corrects. In my view, this risk of a gap between valuation and POET’s actual commercialization is justified, as the company is only in the early stages of its entry into the global AI economy market. My plan, accordingly, will be to initiate new purchases of POET during market phases when the price corrects by 10–20%. From a long-term perspective, my opinion remains that the company is poised for commercial success. My target zone for full profit-taking will therefore be closer to the $25–$30 range.

data.title: VOT Is Struggling In 2026, Consider Core Mid-Cap Growth ETFs
data.description:
Despite the positive impact of rate cuts and investors shift towards mid-caps, Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (VOT) is struggling to generate impressive returns. Year-to-date, VOT is up 1%, underperforming significantly relative to 13% increase of the S&P MidCap 400 Growth Index. The market trends demonstrate that investors have been neglecting upper-middle-market due to its lofty valuations and lower sensitivity to rate cuts. Moreover, VOT’s soft performance is blamed on its portfolio structure compared to the core mid-cap ETFs. Therefore, I downgrade my rating for VOT to hold from buy. I suggest investors to consider the S&P Mid-Cap 400 Growth Index focused ETFs.

Why Does VOT Struggles Despite Favorable Conditions?

While the mid-cap category benefited from a spree of rate cuts and investors shift from expensive large caps to cheap assets, Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF unperformed compared to both large and core-mid caps. Year-to-date, the ETF generated only 1% in price returns. In the past twelve months, its share price was up 23%. On the other hand, year-to-date, the S&P MidCap 400 Growth Index soared 13.5%, extending twelve months surge to 41%. During the same periods, the S&P 500 growth index soared 3% and 47%, respectively.

VOT’s performance signals that its share price has been struggling to respond to both large and mid-cap rallies. In the past few years, investors concentration on mega caps and higher rates negatively impacted its performance. However, despite rate cuts and shift to mid caps in the past few quarters, investors seem less aggressive towards VOT.

The fund offers exposure to an upper end of the mid-cap category. VOT tracks the performance of the CRSP US Mid Cap Growth Index by using full replication technique. The median market cap of its portfolio hovers around $44 billion.

VOT’s soft performance and lack of investor confidence can be blamed on its lofty valuations. The fund currently trades around 35x its trailing earnings, up significantly from 27x of the core mid-cap category. Its P/E ratio is also higher than 33x of large-cap growth category. Similarly VOT’s price to book and sales ratios are also up from core and large-cap ETFs.

In addition, VOT’s portfolio structure lowers its potential to generate market-beating returns. The fund offers nearly a quarter of its portfolio exposure to industrials sector, one of the top-performing sectors so far in 2026. VOT’s industrials concentration is less than the S&P 400 MidCap Growth Index’s 31%. The fund’s top industrials stocks are Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT) and Howmet Aerospace Inc. (HWM). Its exposure to industrials stocks has been positively contributing to its overall performance.

Furthermore, the information technology sector accounts for 23% of its portfolio weight. In the past few years, the most of the tech-driven uptrends were driven by the largest tech stocks, specifically by the mega caps. Whereas, the upper mid-cap tech stocks struggled to deliver gigantic returns. Year-to-date, VOT’s top tech stocks, including DoorDash, Inc. (DASH) and Datadog, Inc. (DDOG) plunged 18% and 5%. Its several other tech stocks are trading in red so far in 2026.

VOT’s consumer cyclical stocks, including Carvana Co. (CVNA), Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. (CMG) and Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) also underperformed in the past few quarters. The financials sector has also been under pressure due to the the impact of rate cuts on margins.

Whereas, so far in 2026, the health care sector ranks among the laggards. VOT’s top health care stocks, including Edwards Lifesciences Corporation (EW) Alnylam Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ALNY), IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. (IDXX) and Zoetis Inc. (ZTS) delivered negative returns. Year-to-date, Alnylam Pharmaceuticals and IDEXX Laboratories are down nearly 20%. VOT has 10% of its weight in the health care sector. The fund’s portfolio also offers a small exposure towards the real estate, communication services, energy and utilities sectors.

Characteristics and Quant Rating

Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF’s portfolio comprises 132 stocks, with the top 10 holdings accounting for 20% of its entire weight. It is a passively managed, index-focused and cap-weighted fund. VOT’s expense ratio of 0.05% is lower compared to the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. The fund’s assets under management hovers around $16 billion, with the three months average shares trading volume of 255 thousand. Its average shares trading volume is up from median of all ETFs of 41 thousand.

VOT earned a hold rating based on the Seeking Alpha analysis, as its price momentum is softer than peers. Moreover, its standard deviation of 14 is up from than the median of all ETFs. Currently, VOT’s per share price is around $282. A higher per share price can lower investors ability to buy shares in a large quantity.

Consider Core Mid-Cap ETFs for Better Growth Prospects

Vanguard S&P Mid-Cap 400 Growth Index Fund ETF (IVOG), which tracks the performance of the S&P MidCap 400 Growth Index by using full replication technique, offers greater growth prospects. Year to date, IVOG’s share price is up 13%. In the last twelve months, the fund’s price appreciated by 47%. Its share price rally is supported by its exposure to the core mid-cap category, which is benefiting the most from investors shift to mid caps from large caps. The fund’s portfolio median market-cap is in the range $12 billion.

In addition, IVOG’s cheap valuations are adding to investor sentiment. The fund currently trades around 27x its trailing twelve months earnings. This is down from both the S&P 500 growth and VOT’s 33x and 34x. Its price to book of 4.5 is also down considerably from its peers.

IVOG’s portfolio structure is also among the biggest catalyst for its price upside, given its greater concentration in the industrials sector, accounting for 31% of its entire weight. Year-to-date, the S&P MidCap 400 Industrials index is up 16%.

Unlike upper-mid-cap tech stocks, the S&P MidCap 400 technology sector is up 28% year-to-date. On the negative side, despite the rate cuts, the S&P MidCap 400 Health Care Index struggled to generate solid returns. Whereas, the utilities, energy, real estate and consumer defensive sectors have been performing well due to low rate conditions and cyclical tailwinds. IVOG earned a buy rating based on the Seeking Alpha quant analysis.

In Conclusion

As the mid-cap category is flourishing due to the rate cuts and investors shift to cheap securities, selecting the right investment is crucial to capitalize on uptrend. Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF is likely to underperform compared to the core mid-cap category due to its lofty valuations and large number of underperforming stocks. Therefore, it might not be a compelling mid-cap vehicle to chase the potential uptrend. Whereas, the core mid-cap investment vehicles, such as Vanguard S&P Mid-Cap 400 Growth Index Fund ETF, may enable investors to capitalize on favorable conditions. Its portfolio structure, cheap valuations and solid share price momentum positions it to capitalize on investors shift to mid caps.

data.title: Tesla: A Very Noisy Q1
data.description:
After the bell on Wednesday, we received first-quarter results from Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). The electric vehicle giant got off to a slow start in 2026, as vehicle deliveries and energy deployments both missed Street estimates. The company ended up beating on the top and bottom lines, but the headline numbers may not be as good as they initially suggest. Thus, management will have to walk a fine line on the call for this initial reaction rally to hold.

Previous Coverage of the Name

I last covered Tesla at the beginning of this month, right after the company released production and delivery figures. While vehicle sales rose more than 21,000 units over the prior year period, Q1 2025 was significantly hampered by factory shutdowns for the Model Y upgrade. Energy storage deployments were well below street estimates, so we've seen analyst estimates tumble a bit in the past three weeks. Since that time, Tesla shares have rallied nearly 10%, mostly taking advantage of an 8% rise in the S&P 500 as U.S./Iran war fears have calmed a bit.

A Look at the Q1 Results

Going into Wednesday's report, Tesla analysts were expecting revenues of $22.20 billion, or about 14.8% growth over the prior-year period. Adjusted earnings per share were forecast to surge almost 29% to $0.35 a share. While vehicle sales were hurt by the expiration of certain EV incentives in late 2025, Tesla stepped up some of its promotional activity earlier this year to drive sales in certain markets. In the graphic below, you can see how Tesla fared against my usual three cases. Dollar values and share counts are in millions, except for earnings per share figures.

The company came in a little ahead of the Seeking Alpha average estimate for $22.20 billion in revenues. I was a bit pessimistic this time around, mostly due to the company's launch of cheaper variants and promotions, but Tesla says it actually recorded higher selling prices and had a nearly $1 billion positive revenue impact over Q1 2025 due to currencies. This seemingly says that the company's sales mix shifted towards more expensive vehicles, while full self-driving sales also increased as the one-time purchase of that feature ended during Q1. Energy revenues were quite weak in the quarter, while services revenues jumped a bit.

Total automotive gross margins, including credit sales, increased by nearly 0.7% from Q4 levels, while energy margins rocketed higher by roughly 11 percentage points. However, management noted in the shareholder letter that it benefited from one-time items related to tariffs for both segments, as well as a one-time benefit in the automotive space due to warranties. There was no figure given for the size of the benefit, but based on that energy margin percentage, it seemingly was quite large. Operating spending did ramp up, and the company recorded some large other expenses without providing details. The 41-cent non-GAAP profit beat the street by 6 cents, but it's hard to determine the quality of that beat until we get more details on some of these non-recurring items.

Balance Sheet and Guidance Update

Tesla produced free cash flow of more than $1.44 billion in Q1. However, it only detailed about $2.5 billion in capex, a very small portion of the at least $20 billion it planned to spend for the full year. That means cash flow numbers for the rest of 2026 will be hampered a bit. Total cash rose by about $700 million to over $44.7 billion, while debt and finance leases increased by more than $850 million to over $9.2 billion. During Q1, a little more than $2 billion was spent on an investment in Elon Musk's AI company, X AI, which is now part of SpaceX (SPACE).

In terms of guidance, there weren't any major updates. Vehicle production will continue based on-demand levels, with volume production of the Semi and Cybercab starting this year. First-generation product lines are being installed for volume production of Optimus robots. There are 3 cities in Texas with unsupervised self-driving taxis currently, although 2 launched in recent days in very limited numbers. In addition to the Bay Area with safety drivers in its taxi service vehicles, Tesla plans near-term expansion to five more major cities.

The Current Valuation Picture

When you look at Tesla, the stock itself looks extremely expensive for an automotive company. The stock finished Wednesday at nearly 191 times its expected adjusted earnings for 2026. As a comparison, the big two traditional U.S. automakers' average numbers are in the single digits times their respective expected earnings. Even if you factor in Tesla as a part-tech company, the valuation is still many times that of the major U.S. tech giants, which mostly currently go for 20 to 30 times their respective annual earnings.

In a different light, in the chart below, you can see how Tesla matches up on a price-to-expected sales basis for 2026 vs. other EV names: Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID), Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), and VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS), XPeng Inc. (XPEV), Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY), BYD Company Limited (BYDDF), and NIO Inc. (NIO). All valuations are as of Wednesday's close.

The average of the seven comparison names was 1.37 times their expected revenues for next year. A number of these other companies are projected to see much higher revenue growth rates than Tesla in the short term. Of course, they are all working off much smaller base numbers. Additionally, Tesla is the only one of these names that has delivered any meaningful amount of profitability and cash flow at times, outside of BYD, but the premium that shares trade for is massive.

As of Wednesday's close, the average price target on the Street was about $416, up about $5 since my previous article. That current average represents more than 7% upside from the day's finish. As you might expect with a stock that has long had such a bull/bear debate, the price target range goes from $125 to $600. There are 48 analysts who have covered the name in the last three months; of them, 23 had some kind of Buy rating, with 18 Holds, and 7 sells.

Final Thoughts and Recommendation

In the end, Tesla announced a very noisy quarter. Expectations went into Wednesday very low after the bad delivery and energy numbers announced a few weeks ago. Thus, Tesla ended up beating very low estimates, although there were some major one-time items that seemingly drove those beats. The balance sheet remains in solid shape, although there looks to be a massive amount of capex spending left for 2026 that could reduce financial flexibility.

TSLA shares jumped initially in the after-hours, but the next few weeks will be dependent on how some of the company's futuristic plans pan out. For the moment, I'm going to keep a sell rating on the stock, just because the valuation doesn't fit the current story. Elon Musk has been hyping things like full self-driving for more than a decade now, and the service remains well behind schedule still. Investors may start to focus more on humanoid robots soon, but until some of these efforts finally bear significant fruit, Tesla remains a very risky name to invest in.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Oracle Of OMAH Wins Round One Vs. Berkshire Hathaway
data.description:
The Solactive VistaShares Berkshire Select Index became operational in 2016 and includes 21 equities. The 21 are comprised of the 20 largest valuation holdings of Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B), along with BRK.B itself.

On the rebalancing dates (February, May, August, and November), 10% of the index is assigned to BRK.B, with the balance of 90% being divided up among the balance 20 securities. Additionally,

...stocks with a weight above 4.5%, including BERKSHIRE HATHAWAY INC-CL B, would collectively have a maximum allocation of 45%. In addition to that, individual stocks are also capped at 20%.

Source: Index Guideline

This index is used for its equity selection by VistaShares Target 15 Berkshire Select Income ETF (OMAH), an actively managed fund that began operations a little over a year ago.

The Fund

OMAH has a two-fold investment objective, with long-term capital appreciation being secondary to earning income. It even has a quantifiable target for its annual income - 15%. Just like the objective, the investment strategy is also two-fold, both parts tied to BRK.B and its holdings (emphasis ours).

1) investing in a portfolio of equity securities based on the Solactive VistaShares Berkshire Select Index ...; and (2) generating income through an options portfolio ...

Source: OMAH Summary Prospectus

The equity portion is managed by the fund sub-advisor, VistaShares LLC, whereas the options portion is managed by the fund advisor. OMAH's equity portfolio serves to achieve its long-term capital appreciation objective. Since OMAH is actively managed, the selections and weightings are not necessarily identical to the index. VistaShares LLC chooses index incumbents based on whether they will help achieve OMAH's objectives. Typically, large-capitalization companies have low volatility, and the index goes with BRK.B's largest 20. Low volatility limits capital gain potential, so at times, OMAH goes shopping outside the index, provided that the chosen ones are still BRK.B holdings (just not in the index). If OMAH steps outside the index, it holds between 21 and 30 equities, which could be a mix of large- and mid-capitalization companies. As of April 17, OMAH held 21 equities, with the top 10 holdings making up close to 62% of the portfolio.

Apple Inc. (AAPL) led the pack, followed by BRK.B, American Express Company (AXP), Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY), and The Coca-Cola Company (KO) rounding out the top 5. OXY makes us nostalgic about one of our "oldie-but-goldie" pieces on this platform from 2019. BRK.B had just entered the OXY scene by financing an OXY acquisition. Anyway, coming back to our protagonist du jour...

OMAH writes options or sells calls on its underlying equities. The premiums from the options portfolio are the main source of OMAH's 15% annual income objective. Having said that, while choosing the option strikes, the advisor also keeps the capital appreciation goal in mind. The options strategy is based on the performance of the underlying holdings.

The Adviser employs different options strategies based on its market outlook, selecting one or a combination of strategies it believes will achieve the Fund’s income target, while also allowing for potential capital appreciation

Source: OMAH Summary Prospectus

Option premiums are heavily dependent on the volatility of the underlying equities, or Vega. That is also factored in when choosing the covered call derivatives for OMAH. A closer look shows that all the options (only a portion are shown below, but we checked) expire fairly soon. As we type this on April 21, 2026, you can see there are many expiring on April 24.

So these are not even the monthly but the weekly options. There are, of course, the regular monthly options, and you can see those for Visa Inc. (V), Chubb Limited (CB), and VeriSign Inc. (VRSN). All in all, the option strategy is similar to most option funds that emphasize shorter-term option selling to get premium rather than enhance defensiveness of the strategy.

Typically, the fund invests around 80% of the net assets in the aforementioned index incumbents or option contracts that provide exposure to the index incumbents. OMAH can also have up to 10% of its net assets in cash or Treasuries.

Performance and Expenses

OMAH has annual expenses of 0.95%, comprising entirely of management fees.

Only a year into operations, OMAH has outperformed BRK.B by close to 15% - thanks to it achieving its annual income target. The option premiums have come to the rescue of the fund.

Verdict

OMAH distributes monthly and currently yields 14.70% (market price $18.52).

Investors should know that it is not always guaranteed that the fund will generate actual income equivalent to the distribution rate. This is especially true when funds sell shorter-term options, which can create heavy whipsaws. The main reason to consider investing is if you want something like BRK.B, but with more income. But it is still apples and oranges. With BRK.B you get a certain portfolio that includes these companies and also lots of unlisted securities like Burlington Northern Santa Fe. You get BRK.B's insurance portfolio, and above all else, you get the cash.

Many on this website are used to phrases like "cash is trash," but in our world, it has an optionality. BRK.B can swoop in on assets at distressed prices, and neither the company nor its founder cares what you think about the cost of sitting idle. The options are also likely to create whipsaws that, of course, run contrary to BRK.B's ultra-long-term thinking. So we would bet that the fund and BRK.B, perform rather differently over the long term. But for those who are in favor of piggybacking on the BRK.B holdings with some option income, this might be a good choice. At least they won't be chasing the tech-heavy indices loaded with garbage LLM investments.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: BILL Holdings: Real AI Concerns, But Priced Accordingly
data.description:
Shares of BILL Holding (BILL) have been suffering from the AI overhang. Trading around the $40 mark, shares trade within imminent reach of the all-time lows, with shares trading at levels seen around its IPO in 2019 but down nearly 90% from a pandemic-induced peak above the $300 mark in 2021.

Like so many growth, software, and technology stocks, the market likely fears the potential of AI to disrupt the business. A big part of the drop is due to simply overvaluation during the pandemic, with the selling substantially starting already ahead of the real emergence of AI, creating further worries for investors. If you are among those fearing AI, many names are probably uninvestable, but otherwise, another software play is up for grabs at just over 2 times (still growing and nearly profitable) sales here.

Other interesting software players potentially disrupted by AI are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Skepticism On Growth

In early February, BILL reported its second quarter result for the fiscal year 2026 as growth in the mid-double digits was in line with the cadence seen in recent times. Total revenues rose some 14% to $415 million, but this includes interest held on funds for customers, with subscription and transaction fee revenues up 17% to $375 million. Interest received on funds held for customers fell a bit, largely due to lower interest income, as these funds (around $4.0 billion) were pretty stable from the year before.

The company is effectively starting to break even, with quarterly GAAP operating losses of $18 million being down in a small fashion from the same quarter a year before and largely in line with the first quarter. This follows a similar-sized provision for credit losses, as net losses were much less pronounced at $2 million. This follows large interest income received on net cash balances on the balance sheet.

Adjusted basic earnings rose by fifteen cents to $0.91 per share, but this kindly adjusts for a pre-tax stock-based compensation expense of $0.64 per share, as well as a $0.23 per share depreciation & amortization charge, as otherwise earnings are seen as minimally positive.

More of the same is seen for the year, with total sales seen up 12-13% to a midpoint of $1.64 billion, with non-GAAP earnings seen between $2.33-$2.41 per share. With earnings on these metrics already totaling $1.25 per share in the first half of the year, that implies $1.08-$1.16 per share expectations for the second half of the year. That is not necessarily a bad thing, with stock-based compensation expenses seen trending down a bit, at least on a relative basis, as that could be helpful to the bottom line but hurt adjusted earnings ironically.

About Expectations

Following some buybacks, the share count of the business stands at 100 million shares, granting the company a $4.0 billion equity valuation at the $40 mark. This includes a $2.24 billion cash and equivalents position, with the liability side of the balance sheet revealing a $330 million regular debt load and $1.50 billion in convertible senior notes outstanding. These convertible notes are far out of the money (way above the $100 mark) but provide interest-free debt until 2030.

So the net balance reveals about $400 million in net cash, with a high net interest income received on this (at least until 2030), reducing the enterprise valuation to $3.6 billion, or perhaps a bit less. Based on the outlook, the company trades just over 2 times sales, with little to minimal earnings to be provided for.

So How Safe Is This?

The idea of BILL is to provide a money-saving solution for small- and medium-sized businesses. These include automation and simplification of back-office operations related to generating and processing invoices, obtaining approvals, and even making payments, which frankly seem like textbook examples of tasks to be disrupted by AI. Other efforts include eliminating disparate systems, improving visibility, and eliminating siloed workflows.

While that is very true, note that BILL's technology was designed to automate this. This means that we are not moving from tasks from humans to AI, but frankly the disruption is that technology could become an AI task, potentially reducing the benefits (savings) from automation here.

Moreover, called the champion of small and mid-sized businesses, the focus on these smaller businesses creates more insulation from AI as a threat, although this seems more of a timing thing rather than a definitive roadblock. However, the (implied) fees charged by the business are still substantial, with revenues of around $1.5 billion being equal to over 40 basis points in relation to $350 billion in payment volumes processed. This includes over 130 million transactions processed, nearly 500k businesses using its software, and some 8 million people using the software, one way or another.

So far, in user metrics, no real deceleration is seen here, which does not say that the AI threat is not credible; it might just not translate into real competition and customer losses here, as that will be the focus for investors going forward.

Some Perspective

A little over three years ago, I concluded that BILL was not paying its bills. While sales multiples were still seen near 10 times sales, it was a lack of real operating leverage that prevented me from getting on board, with shares cut in half ever since, marking dramatic returns, certainly on a relative basis.

This came after the business had seen growth, albeit slower growth, with the company having seen huge valuation multiples in the past. Trading around 15 times annualized sales at the time of the IPO in 2019, 50 times at the peak, to just over 2 times sales today. Of course, poor allocation decisions were made at the peak, including a $2.5 billion deal for Divvy and a $625 million deal for Invoice2go, as two examples. These, however, were not necessarily poor deals, with shares sold or issued at peak valuation to pay for them.

Trading around 9 times sales in 2023, while growth was still very high and modest realistic losses were reported, valuations have compressed a great deal further. This came in between further growth in sales and continued declines in operating asset valuations, although a substantial part of the net cash holdings have been spent on buybacks recently.

Like so many software names, valuations have absolutely imploded, all while continued sales growth and operating leverage are seen here. This makes it seem appealing to me, as now might be the time to go bottom fishing across the industry. If AI will disrupt all technology, cheap is never cheap enough, but if software names can compete or even benefit from AI, great buying opportunities are seen here. Some real leverage is necessary, as BILL has an advantage in terms of the structuring of convertible notes, providing real interest income on minimal net cash balances, an advantage seen disappearing in 2030.

BILL Holdings seems like a textbook play to be disrupted. The integrated nature of the activities, the relatively smaller amount spent by the average business, and the fact that it focuses on SMEs and involves actual payments mean disruption might take longer than perhaps feared. This is potentially creating some runway for BILL meanwhile, and might also be the potential with Hellman & Friedman reportedly interested in buying the business earlier this year.

However, fearing the disruptive 'potential' of the business, I am still erring a bit on the cautious side, favoring other parts of the software complex, although I am keen to keep an eye on BILL's shares in 2026 here.

data.title: Moody's: The Bears Are Losing Their Arguments (Q1 Earnings Review)
data.description:
Introduction

It has been four years since I started writing on Seeking Alpha, and last month I had my first opportunity to be featured on the Portfolio Pulse video series on SA, comparing Moody's and S&P Global. Moody's (MCO) has just released its Q1 2026 earnings report, and it is therefore time to update our assessment.

The first time I wrote on the stock, it was because I reverse-engineered Buffett's investment thesis, which clearly hinged on the compounding quality of the company and its extremely strong moat. However, back then the stock was trading at fwd PE close to 40, which made it too expensive. A year later, I came back and considered the stock a buy finally because the outlook had improved. In particular, we were seeing private credit growth as well as a debt maturity wall that was approaching. I saw Moody's as an indirect beneficiary of the AI buildout. Then, a few months ago, I ran a head-to-head comparison between the two undisputed leaders in debt ratings as AI disruption fears were sending both MCO and SPGI down. I said that MCO was a better pick over SPGI, and this is, so far, aging well, as Moody's is proving to be a bit more resilient than its peers.

My thesis was that AI fears were overblown. In particular, Moody's analytics business is more insulated than SPGI because it provides data that are used by loan models with regulatory approval. Let's then turn to Moody's report and see the new financial figures released.

Moody's Q1 2026 Earnings Review

Was I surprised by the report and by the fact that Moody's beat both top and bottom line estimates? The first two months of the quarter saw high issuance volumes, fueled by the AI buildout and M&A activity. March, as we all know, was a hard month where credit spreads widened, creating some softness in new debt issuance. This is why investors were more concerned about the outlook than about the past quarter.

But let's see the main numbers that Moody's reported. First of all, total revenues were up 8% to $2.08B. However, the company's adj. EPS grew by 13% to $4.33, beating the consensus estimate by $0.11. The adj. The operating margin improved by 150 bps to 53.2%, thanks to the strong performance of Moody's Analytics. As a result, the company's operating cash flow grew 24% YoY to $939M, and the FCF was up by 26% to $844M. Moody's proved once again that it is a compounding powerhouse.

Moody's has two segments - MIS and MA - and I think this report can help us understand a bit more about how valuable and healthy they are.

MIS (Moody's Investors Service) was the debated segment. In fact, while corporate bond issuance was strong, as well as M&A activity while private credit kept generating higher rating volume. But some investors were cautious because of tough comparables (especially when considering leveraged loan issuance) and the fact that March was a bad month for bond issuance due to rising yields.

But I think this quarter shows that MIS is less cyclical than people think. It's true that corporate bond issuance can fluctuate, but MIS also makes money by charging monitoring fees on the companies it monitors.

When it comes to MA (Moody’s Analytics), we see that it was business as usual. The ARR grew 8% to $3.6B, driven by the Decision Solutions division, which grew 12%. The most important change is that Moody's announced that Christina Kosmowsky will become MA's CEO. Interestingly, she has worked at Salesforce, which makes me think that Moody's was looking for someone who understands software and platform economics. In other words, this could be a signal that MA is not just a data delivery business.

AI disruption fears are now part of the risk framework that investors need to use. Just a few days ago, Moody's announced that it was integrating its credit and compliance workflows into Claude. This is the exact opposite of what disruption should be. Moody's owns valuable proprietary data and models. These are now embedded in Claude, which means that Moody's has more distribution. Then, Moody's launched on the AWS Marketplace Agentic Solutions. This means that Moody's AI-powered analytics are available directly where most banks operate. If you think about it, the moat is not being disrupted, but it is actually leveraged as it extends into new spaces. The risk that remains is on seat-based subscriptions. If AI increases productivity rates, then fewer workers will be required to do the same analytical jobs. But Moody's has shown that it is building AI-native interfaces, and this could truly offset the negative impact of headcount reduction. In any case, since AI is about leveraging data, I think businesses with valuable proprietary datasets will almost inevitably benefit from this big technological revolution.

Finally, let's look at debt maturities. Below, we see that both U.S. and EMEA corporate bonds and loans (ex-financials) are going to hit big walls, which will drive refinancing needs. This is a big tailwind for the company.

What matters more than all that we have said above is the guidance. Moody's management was confident that its FY26 guidance will be met. Both MA and MIS are expected to grow their revenues in the high-single-digit range, with operating margins, respectively, at 34.5% and 65%. This is why we are seeing that Moody's share repurchases have been increased by 25% and are now expected to reach $2.5B during FY26.

Moody's Valuation

Moody's is now trading at a fwd PE of 28 when we consider that its share price is $470 and its FY26 EPS could be around $16.70. Historically, MCO has traded at higher multiples, but the AI disruption overhang remains, and it's still keeping the multiple in the high 20s. While we are still waiting for SPGI to report its earnings, we have seen that MSCI has been able to report further growth in its data analytics business. So far, it is hard to see the bear scenario that some imagined.

A few months ago, I said Moody's outlook looked brighter than what many thought due to four reasons: better AI resilience thanks to its MA models that are required by the regulator, the absence of spin-off noise (S&P Global is spinning off the mobility division), strong margin durability, and declining business cyclicality. Each of these aspects is confirmed, if not even strengthened (in particular, AI resilience).

My rating, therefore, remains a buy, since I think that Moody's could trade back in the low 30x earnings, meaning that it could trade somewhere between $520 and $550.

data.title: Cummins: Running On All Engines, But Valuation Makes It A 'Hold'
data.description:
Shares of Cummins (CMI) are among the key derivative plays in the current AI boom. The company is seeing real signs of a growth acceleration, even as the headline results somewhat disguise this uptick in the business here.

Reported sales are flattish due to soft trucking/engine markets, but underlying growth in data centers and power solutions is doing fine, setting the company up for strong growth in 2026, both in sales and earnings.

That is to be applauded, yet investors have gotten too carried away, in my view, with substantial premiums awarded to a pretty decent business. These growth engines only make up 10%-20% of the business, making me naturally a bit cautious here.

Other interesting growth players tied to the electrical theme are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Headline Results Understate Growth

Early in February, Cummins reported its results for the fiscal year 2025, a year in which revenues of $33.7 billion were down just over a percent on the year before. Note that these revenues exclude intersegmental sales.

These revenues are reported across five segments, the largest of which is a $12.4 billion distribution business, up 9% on the year before, with solid margin expansion seen, up 250 basis points to nearly 15% of sales.

Driven by sluggish operating environments in automotive and related markets, an $8.1 billion engine and $8.6 billion components business saw real revenue declines. Of interest to the market are the promising segments, mostly power. Power system sales rose some 17% to $4.1 billion, with fat EBITDA margins of 22% and change up a full four points. This is completed by a smaller $460 million Accelera business, one posting EBITDA losses roughly twice the revenue amount, this being the electrolyzer business.

In terms of profitability metrics, GAAP operating profits rose modestly to $4.0 billion, as impairment charges made GAAP net earnings fall to $2.8 billion, for earnings equal to $20.50 per share. This came after a $3.28 per share post-tax charge in relation to Accelera (on top of the operational losses), and while this could be a promising business line, it is a real detractor to results here. As a result, the company is pursuing strategic alternatives.

Powering On

The 17% growth number in the power segment in 2025 for the year looks strong in relation to a 7% increase in that metric in the final quarter. However, if internal sales are included, the fourth-quarter increase in sales came in at 11%.

That is a key growth metric that investors are looking forward to; these sales are, of course, heavily tied to the boom in data centers. Note that data centers are also contributing to the distribution segment of Cummins, a larger business, yet slower-growing business.

Growth here is key, as that is what has been driving share price momentum, yet overall data center sales (split across both distribution and power) still make up just a minority of company-wide sales here.

Further growth in this is key to driving overall growth further; yet another driver is the strategic evaluation of Accelera, a decision attributed to deteriorating conditions in the electrolyzer market and hydrogen market. This comes from market forces but also from a reduction in government incentives. In fact, the company incurred a negative $896 million EBITDA result in this segment in 2025, a dreadful result. In fact, with some 139 million shares outstanding, these EBITDA losses alone are equal to $6.50 per share, with earnings otherwise coming in close to $27 per share!

Net debt of $4.0 billion is very modest, trailing adjusted EBITDA of $5.8 billion by a wide margin. The company guided for real growth in 2026, with sales seen up between 3% and 8%, but moreover, EBITDA margins are seen up between 1 and 2 points to 17%-18% of sales.

This seems to be largely driven by the operating performance, as a reduction in the losses at Accelera could provide additional growth. With adjusted earnings seen around $24 per share on a stand-alone basis, earnings could easily rise to the higher twenties, based on the organic growth and limiting or eliminating losses at Accelera. That is rather comforting amidst a strong balance sheet here.

Expectations Rise

Still just a stock trading in the low $200s early in 2024, Cummins commanded a low valuation as investors feared the positioning of the firm in light of the electrification wave. Only later did real momentum show up here, with shares trading near the $400 mark early in 2025 to rise to a fresh all-time high of $640 per share here. This came largely from higher expectations awarded to the shares.

Based on earnings power around the $20 mark, or even a bit higher recently, earnings must have risen from about 10-12 times to levels in the mid-20s, or low-20s, based on the revised outlook for 2026. This is driven by a stronger North American on-highway truck market and continued strength in the data center market.

In fact, shares have increased by a factor of about 6 times from levels around the $100 mark a decade ago. Ever since Cummins has nearly doubled sales, it has grown operating margins a few points while retiring about one in every five shares outstanding. While these achievements are very solid, it is clear that valuation multiple inflation played a key role in this as well.

What Now?

The reality is that 2026 could become a key year. Driven by an anticipated recovery in automotive and truck markets, the company sees a return to modest growth in engines and components. This is complemented by stronger growth in distribution revenues, which has become the largest segment of the business with over a third of sales, driven by engine/generator sets delivered, complemented by power revenues typically ranging a few MW.

The reality is that Cummins feels really overextended, still being a $400 stock in September, trading around the $600 mark early this year. After falling to a low around the $500 mark in March, shares have seen a swift 25% return in the time frame of just a few weeks.

Obviously, some higher expectations have emerged in the stock, and while this momentum is seen in the 2026 guidance, most of the gains have been driven by valuation inflation, which makes me a bit concerned. Moreover, while data centers are typically equipped with backup generators, and Cummins definitely benefits from that, that is not a default situation going forward.

Less critical applications, such as AI, make it so that some of these data centers are built without all the redundancy features, such as those offered by Cummins. Nonetheless, the company has savvily positioned itself to work together with Eaton (ETN) in this area, providing a greater one-stop solution for clients.

However, multiple long-term positive trends persist, including an anticipated recovery in truck markets, the rise of battery energy systems and power systems for data centers, and more stringent low-NOx rules for 2027 (issued by the EPA). Regarding data centers, which have really been a driver for the business, data center revenues across segments grew a healthy 35% last year to $3.5 billion, for just over 10% of sales. A continuation of such growth, of course, alone creates a 3%-4% tailwind to organic growth, this being the key growth engine of the business.

That is precisely the issue in my view. Even if 90% of the core business deserves a market multiple of, let's say, 18 times, which might already be a stretch. If the remaining 10% of the data center business could fetch a 30-40 times earnings multiple, the overall earnings multiple awarded to the business should only be 19-20, or even less. With the business trading at a low- to mid-20 times forward earnings, they are clearly extended in my view, although a period of stagnation and/or pullback might easily be used to reconsider that stance here.

data.title: PCN: Efficient Portfolio Strategy And Attractive Outlook
data.description:
Overview

2026 was off to a rough start as the equity markets pulled back from their highs. Similarly, the debt markets faced headwinds as fears around defaults continued to rise. This created a scenario where even high-quality income funds declined in value and failed to serve as a buffer against the volatility of equities. The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund (PCN) also experienced a pullback and now trades at an attractive valuation. At the time of my last coverage, I issued a buy rating due to the valuation and dividend sustainability at the time. The fund has provided updated reporting, so I wanted to revisit PCN to reassess its value proposition and outlook going forward.

When I previously covered PCN, the fund traded at a small premium to NAV of 5.89%. Following the slight pullback in share price, the fund now trades at a smaller premium to NAV of 3.71%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that the fund now trades at one of the most attractive price-to-NAV levels over the last five years. For instance, PCN has traded at an average premium to NAV of 14.9% over the last five years. However, investors should also be aware that the current price-to-NAV level is a direct reflection of some of the challenges the fund is now facing.

PCN now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11.2% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. Despite the market uncertainty over the last quarter, the fund's reporting still indicates that the portfolio strategy can efficiently out-earn the distributions that are being paid. So despite its risks, the underlying NAV should remain resilient going forward. PCN's performance is likely to improve if we assume that interest rates will continue to trend lower over the next twelve months.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, PCN now has total managed assets of $1.08B that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The fund's primary goal is to generate a high level of current income, which means that the performance will not be correlated to the equity markets. Instead, PCN utilizes several debt-focused securities that rely on a borrower's ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance. The fund leans most heavily on high-yield credit investments at 32.24% of assets. However, the fund also invests in different instruments, like emerging market, non-USD developed, and non-agency mortgage securities, to help balance out the risks from the high-yield credit investments.

Furthermore, the fund also provides some exposure across different industries to help mitigate concentration risks. For instance, the fund is most heavily allocated towards technology investments at 12.20% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to healthcare and media cable positions, which accounts for 5.11% and 4.35% of assets, respectively. I think it's worth noting that the exposure to technology positions can potentially increase the risk profile for the fund, since we are in a period where the market is punishing software companies. The rising threat of AI is causing investors to question the moat of many technology companies. While there are no specific signs this is negatively impacting PCN, it is something for investors to keep in mind.

Additionally, the fund provides some exposure to global regions outside of the U.S. Since the fund still leans most heavily on U.S. securities, I wouldn't classify PCN as a dedicated global fund, but it is an investment that can be attractive for investors that want to get a bit of diversity away from a reliance on the U.S. debt markets. The fund invests across different regions of Europe, Asia, and South America, for example. These emerging market investments also have the potential to boost a higher yield for the fund.

In terms of maturities, the fund tends to lean more heavily on short-dated securities. I consider short-dated securities to have a maturity that is less than 5 years out. The benefit of these shorter-dated securities is that they potentially provide better resilience against fluctuations in interest rates, but they also require management to actively replace their income with new investments more frequently. Conversely, the longer-dated securities tend to be more reactive to high-interest rates. The breakdown by maturity is as follows:

  • Short-Dated (0-5 Years): 66.14%.
  • Long-Dated (5-20+ Years): 33.87%.

Performance Expectations

Since my last coverage, the fund has also released an updated semi-annual report that provides some clarity into the earnings dynamics. Net investment income amounted to $0.58 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.16 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the next half of the period, PCN is aligned to generate total net investment income that is on par with its typical range. This can be an indication that the portfolio strategy is resilient through different market cycles and is capable of consistently generating income. What makes PCN stand out among the PIMCO funds is that the net investment income levels are usually strong enough to support most of the distributions being paid out.

This dynamic means that PCN is less reliant on net realized gains to bridge any gaps in its earnings. So even if the positions within PCN's portfolio decline, the net investment income collected can still serve as a buffer and support the majority of distributions being paid. However, this doesn't entirely protect the fund against market declines. For instance, the fund still hasn't fully recovered from the large net realized losses that were experienced through 2022, which means the NAV is still below its prior highs. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $12.76 per share.
  • 2021: $14.54 per share.
  • 2022: $11.60 per share.
  • 2023: $11.14 per share.
  • 2024: $11.40 per share.
  • 2025: $11.82 per share.
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $11.94 per share.

So while the fund has some potential for capital appreciation, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the distributions being paid. For instance, PCN's share price has now declined by a little more than 15.4% over the last decade, even though traditional market indices have rallied to all-time highs over the last decade. Meanwhile, the fund's total return when including all distributions jumps up to 117.2% over the same time frame.

Looking at the performance chart above, we can see that there was a time frame where the fund's share price did appreciate in value. Coincidentally, this was when interest rates were a lot lower and served as a more favorable operating environment for the debt securities within its portfolio. So while the share price is suppressed, this could be a great time to accumulate shares in anticipation of interest rates trending lower over the next twelve months.

A lower interest rate environment can improve operating efficiency for PCN's existing positions and provide relief across the entire debt market. Similarly, a more favorable cost of debt could possibly spark an influx of new borrowers in the market. This would provide management with more opportunities to invest in new positions that can improve its baseline earnings and help the NAV grow over time.

Risks

While lower interest rates can serve as a catalyst in the future, a scenario where interest rates remain elevated can also be one of the primary risks for PCN. Although the Fed initiated several cuts to interest rates through 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided that interest rates would be left unchanged. I believe there are two primary risks to interest rates remaining high:

  1. Increased potential to suffer from borrower defaults.
  2. Limited growth opportunities that can replace maturing securities.

PCN has a sizable allocation to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating below BBB are classified as below investment grade and historically have the highest potential for defaults. These are generally borrowers that have less consistent cash flows or that may be more reliant on debt financing to fuel operations. According to CEF Data, the fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 59.19%.
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 34.14%.
  • Not Rated: 6.98%.

The fund also uses leverage to help amplify its earnings. Total effective leverage now amounts to 21.88% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $238M. While interest rates remain elevated, this leverage can negatively impact PCN's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities. Therefore, PCN may have to allocate a greater portion of its earnings towards interest expenses. Similarly, leverage can amplify downside movements if allocated to securities that decline in value. To be fair, though, leverage can amplify upside movements once interest rates trend lower and the value of PCN's positions rises.

However, the high-interest rate environment means that there are simply fewer borrowers seeking debt financing right now. This can limit growth opportunities for PCN, and the rate of maturing securities can outpace new investments at some point. It is hard to estimate where interest rates will be twelve months from now, but it is possible that they will remain elevated. This dynamic can continue to suppress PCN's share price and limit capital appreciation.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1125 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.2%. Assuming the fund keeps payouts unchanged for the next twelve months, this would result in an annual distribution total of $1.35 per share. Referring back to the same reporting, it is clear that PCN is capable of generating earnings that outpace the distribution amount. For instance, PCN reported total earnings of $1.55 per share for the 2025 period.

Looking over the history of the fund, PCN has kept payouts very consistent over the last decade. This reinforces that PCN's portfolio strategy is efficient at generating income that can support payouts. This consistency makes PCN a great option for investors seeking income stability within their portfolio. Even though the fund hasn't provided any sort of traditional dividend growth, this consistency allows investors to create their own dividend growth through the reinvestment of distributions or by adding new capital to their position over time.

However, the latest UNII report indicates that the fund isn't quite covering the distribution entirely on a YTD basis. On a YTD basis, the fund is only covering the distributions with a coverage rate of 89.37%. So while the historical data shows us that management keeps payouts the same, there may also be instances where this decision to keep payouts the same is negatively hurting the NAV. Therefore, PCN's NAV has the risk of trending lower if net realized gains aren't able to make up the difference in earnings.

This is why the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that a portion of the last payout was supported by 'paid-in surplus,' otherwise known as return of capital. Return of capital distributions offer some tax efficiency, but this is because the fund is paying out this portion of its distributions directly from its NAV rather than earnings. So if the fund becomes more reliant on return of capital, it can exacerbate the rate at which the NAV declines.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain a buy rating on PCN at this time. The fund trades at an attractive price-to-NAV valuation, and even though this may be directly influenced by challenging market conditions, the fund has also demonstrated its ability to remain resilient. PCN's net investment income continues to fall at a level that is on par with its usual range, even through challenging market conditions. I believe that future interest rate cuts will serve as a growth catalyst for the fund, and this can be an attractive time to accumulate shares in anticipation of that. The fund's decision to keep payouts consistent while also growing its NAV over the last few years is a demonstration that a recovery is slowly happening.

data.title: MFA Financial Under The Microscope: Baby Bonds, Preferred Stocks And Credit Quality
data.description:
Today, we take a look at MFA Financial (MFA). We will go deep into the company, its credit quality, as well as its financial instruments and their yields.

MFA's current market capitalization is close to $1.03 billion. The total assets at the end of 2025 were around $13.04 billion, and the total debt was approximately $10.93 billion. The baby bonds represent $190 million, and the nominal value of the preferred stocks is approximately $475 million. The most significant portion of MFA's investment portfolio is close to 43% in Non-QM Loans and 27% in Agency MBS.

The recourse leverage is 2.5x. The key metric in evaluating mREITs is recourse leverage. At 2.5x, MFA is significantly more conservative than the average US bank, which typically operates at around 6x.

Baby Bonds

MFA has two baby bonds: (MFAN) and (MFAO):

Both are currently trading above par. You can see for yourself the difference in baby bond yields to worst from our last article here. Currently, MFAN offers a yield to worst of around 6.55%, and MFAO offers a yield to worst of around 5.78%, while in our last article, they were offering over 9%. The call date of MFAN has passed, while the call date of MFAO is 08/15/2026. Both bonds have ~3 years to maturity with maturity dates MFAN: 02/15/2029 and MFAO: 08/15/2029. In the next chart are shown the potential yields to worst and prices of both bonds:

MFAN:

MFAO:

Preferred Stocks

And below are shown the preferred stocks of the company:

(MFA.PR.C) with a floating rate and (MFA.PR.B) with a fixed rate. Both stocks are trading below par at the time of writing. And both are callable - their call dates have passed. The fixed-rate MFA.PR.B offers a yield to worst of around 9.23% - a competitive yield even compared to other fixed-rate preferred stocks that trade below par and are callable in the mortgage sector.

Here you can see the YTW vs. prices of MFA.PR.B chart:

At the same time, MFA.PR.C offers an attractive opportunity for investors as it switched from a fixed to a floating dividend in March 2025 with a significant spread of over 5.6% over SOFR. At the current market price below par, the instrument provides a yield to worst of around 10.17%, combining high current income with the potential for capital gains upon a return to parity.

Credit Quality

To assess the credit quality of MFA, we use several key metrics, which you can view below.

The market capitalization of cоmmon plus prеferred stock divided by the par value of the company's unsеcured bonds to dеtermine the "baby bond buffer." MFA has about 7.93 times as much common and preferred stock as bonds.

The dividеnds pаid on commоn and prefеrred stock are dividеd by the intеrest that will bе pаid on the bаby bоnds. Hеre, the ratio is close to 10.51.

The company's tоtal assets dividеd by the mаrket cаpitalization of cоmmon and prefеrred stоck givеs an idеa of ​​hоw indеbted the cоmpany is in compаrison. Here, the ratio is close to 8.66.

Conclusion

MFA demonstrates solid fundamentals for an mREIT, with a diversified portfolio centered on Non-QM loans and Agency MBS and a moderate level of leverage. The baby bonds currently offer yields to worst of around 6%, while the preferred stocks offer yields in excess of 9% and 10%, and a credit buffer of over 7.93 provides reasonable confidence in the company’s ability to service its debt. Even if you decide to invest in the fixed-rate preferred stock that looks the most attractive with a yield of over 9% and a price below par, it is good to know that 10%+ is not such a "safe" yield to worst. Such an investment requires active monitoring and a willingness to realize a loss if necessary if the fundamental picture changes significantly.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market's Plunge Offers Investors A Discount (Upgrade)
data.description:
The last article that I wrote about Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM), was published back in July of last year. In that article, I reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. Even though I acknowledged that the company was achieving impressive growth when it came to revenue, profits, and store count, I argued that shares were priced at levels that were difficult to justify. I wouldn't go so far as to take a bearish stance on it. But looking back, I probably should have. Shares have plummeted 53.2% since that article came out. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up 4.5%. This plunge, it seems, has been the result of slowing growth, combined with the fact that shares were not cheap at that time. However, I would argue that the market has overreacted to this. Based on the data that's currently available, with management forecasting continued growth on the top line this year and with shares trading at levels that are attractively priced, I believe that upgrading it to a soft ‘buy’ makes a lot of sense here.

Even though I am making the decision to upgrade the company right now, it is possible that my mind could change as new data comes in. Interestingly enough, that should not be too far off into the future. Management has made clear to investors that, on April 29th, the company will announce financial results for the first quarter of its 2026 fiscal year. Revenue is expected to rise, but there is the belief among analysts that overall profits will worsen. Although this would be disappointing in and of itself, guidance provided by management for 2026 in its entirety suggests that results for the year won't really be any different than they were in 2025.

Shares Are Looking Tasty

Odds are, you already know what Sprouts Farmers Market is and what it does. But if you don't, it's helpful to know that the business operates as a specialty natural and organic food retailer that has successfully carved out a nice niche for itself in recent years. Historically, the institution prioritizes perishables over non-perishables. About 57% of revenue came from the perishables category last year. Unlike many retailers that are out there, the business is not afraid to innovate in interesting ways. This includes its own Sprouts-branded products that now account for just over 25% of the company's revenue. This is particularly intriguing since owning its own brands means that the business has greater operational control and that margins have a tendency to be higher than if the firm were to essentially act as a middleman, like it does for most of its revenue stream.

Over the last few years now, the firm has achieved some rather strong growth. From 2023 through 2025, revenue for the institution jumped from $6.84 billion to $8.81 billion. A big driver behind the growth for the company has been a rise in the number of locations that it has in operation. Over the last three years, the business has gone from having 407 stores to having 477. But that's not all. Comparable store sales have also achieved rather remarkable results. In fact, over the last three years now, we have seen them increase quite a bit.

In 2025, as an example, they ended up coming in at 7.3% above what they were a year earlier. Management mentioned in their latest earnings call that the company continues to benefit in this regard from continued innovation and product differentiation. On the product side of things, management mentioned that throughout the year they introduced more than 7,000 new items to their stores, including more than 600 new products under its Sprouts brand. The rolling out of its loyalty program is also almost certainly proving to be a benefit to the business. As great as that is, in the most recent quarter on its own, the company saw comparable sales growth of just 1.6%. This is actually indicative of a meaningful deceleration in growth. We don't really have a lot of detail here. But management did mention that the macroeconomic environment remains uneven and that consumers are increasingly changing their preferences toward value-centric opportunities. Considering that natural and healthy options have a tendency to be more expensive than what else is out there, it's not shocking to see weakness in that regard.

As revenue for the company increases, profitability rises also. Net income more than doubled from $258.9 million to $523.7 million over the last three years. But that's not all, though. We saw operating cash flow expand from $465.1 million to $716 million. And if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $569.5 million to $871.2 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $528.2 million to $843.9 million.

The good news is that management expects revenue growth to at least continue throughout this year. They anticipate sales growth of between 4.5% and 6.5%. Unfortunately, pretty much all of this will come from an increase in the number of stores and operations. And that's because comparable store sales growth will range between being negative 1% and positive 1%. This will come from the addition of 40 new stores that management will allocate a large portion of its $280 million to $310 million of capital expenditures toward.

Achieving this target would likely translate to earnings per share, according to the company, of between $5.28 and $5.44. If the firm can achieve the midpoint of this range, that would mean net profits of $522.8 million. This would likely mean adjusted operating cash flow of $869.7 million and EBITDA of $842.4 million. In the chart above, you can see what this would mean for the valuation of the business compared to how the company is valued using results from 2025. Honestly, I consider these to be very attractive multiples on their own. But the stock isn't cheap just on an absolute basis. It's also a bit near the cheaper end of the spectrum when it comes to other similar enterprises. In that same chart, you can see how the firm compares to five other similar enterprises. Only one of them ended up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. Two were cheaper when it came to the price-to-adjusted operating cash flow approach. And lastly, three of them were cheaper on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. Overall, however, I would say that I am slightly bullish on the valuation of the company in its totality.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that, on April 29th, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts do expect continued revenue growth, with sales forecasted to rise from $2.24 billion to $2.33 billion. Again, the heavy lifting here should come from the rise in store count. Earnings per share, meanwhile, have been forecasted to come in at around $1.68. That would mean net profits of approximately $163.9 million. This compares to the $180 million in net profit that worked out to a profit per share of $1.83 that the company reported for the first quarter of 2025. The table below shows certain cash flow metrics for the company for the first quarter of 2025. If profitability does worsen, I would expect those to show some weakness also.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right this moment, I believe that the market has overreacted to what is clearly a case of slowing growth. The stock was priced at levels before that were difficult to justify, and we have seen play out exactly the kind of scenario that I feared. But the good news is that, given how cheap the stock is, I would say that it is most certainly worthy of an upgrade to a ‘buy’ right now.
